title: Chapter 8 Master Quiz
description: Test your mastery of Advanced OOP: Static, Encapsulation, and Inheritance.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a normal Attribute and a `static` Attribute?
options: Normal attributes are faster to read.
Normal attributes belong to individual Object instances. Static attributes belong to the Class Blueprint and are shared identically across ALL instances.
Static attributes cannot be changed once declared.
Normal attributes can only hold numbers.
questions.explanation: Static means there is only ONE of this variable in memory, shared globally by every Object built from that Class.

questions.question: If `class Car { static int totalCars = 0; }` is run. How do you correctly read this variable from the `main` method?
options: Car myCar = new Car(); return myCar.totalCars;
questions.explanation: Because it belongs to the Blueprint, you access it using the Class Name itself, without needing to `new` an object first.

questions.question: Is it possible to run a `static` Method without ever creating an Object of that class?
questions.explanation: Yes! The `main` method is exactly this. It's static, so Java runs it instantaneously when the file executes.

questions.question: What happens if a variable is declared as `private`?
options: It is only accessible to methods written inside that exact same Class.
It is hidden from the console.
questions.explanation: Private locks the data down. External classes (like the Main class) cannot directly read or write to it.

questions.question: Why is it an industry standard to make all your object Attributes `private`?
options: To protect the object's internal state from being maliciously or accidentally corrupted by external code.
To make the code run faster.
questions.explanation: It's all about safety. Don't let someone set your dog's age to -500!

questions.question: If your attributes are `private`, how is the outside world supposed to read them?
options: By declaring the external class as a friend.
questions.explanation: A Getter is a controlled, public "doorway" that safely passes a copy of the private data outward.

questions.question: Which of these is a standard Getter for `private int score;`?
options: public void getScore(int newScore) { score = newScore; }
questions.explanation: It starts with `get`, is `public`, returns an `int`, and outputs the variable.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using a controlled 'Setter' method instead of making a variable public?
options: It runs faster on the CPU.
You can write validation logic inside the Setter (e.g., checking if the incoming number is negative) to block bad data.
questions.explanation: Setters let you establish rules! "I will let you update the bank balance, but ONLY if the amount is greater than zero."

questions.question: The architectural concept of hiding your private data and exposing it only through secure public methods is called ______.
questions.explanation: Encapsulation acts as a protective capsule around your object's internal machinery.

questions.question: What is 'Inheritance' in Java OOP?
options: A feature that allows a 'Child' class to automatically acquire all the attributes and methods of a 'Parent' class.
A feature that deletes old code.
A way to pass data between arrays.
questions.explanation: Inheritance defines an "IS-A" relationship (e.g., A Dog IS-A Animal) and allows massive code reuse.

questions.question: Which keyword establishes Inheritance between a Child and a Parent class?
questions.explanation: Example: `class Dog extends Animal`.

questions.question: If a `Cat` class extends the `Animal` class, adding a `meow()` method to the Cat class automatically adds the `meow()` method to the Animal class too.
questions.explanation: Inheritance is one-way. Children get everything from the Parent, but Parents do not get things from the Child.

questions.question: If `class Car extends Vehicle` and `Vehicle` has `public void startEngine()`, what happens if you run `Car c = new Car(); c.startEngine();`?
options: Compiler Error since Car doesn't have that method written.
It runs perfectly, Car inherited it.
questions.correctAnswer: It runs perfectly, Car inherited it.
questions.explanation: Because Car extends Vehicle, it instantly possesses the `startEngine` behavior without needing to type it.

questions.question: What does it mean to 'Override' a method?
options: To delete it from the parent class.
To write a new method in the Child class with the EXACT same name and parameters as the Parent class, entirely replacing the Parent's version.
To call a method twice at the same time.
To change a public method to private.
questions.explanation: Overriding allows a Child to customize or replace an inherited behavior it didn't like.

questions.question: Why do developers write `@Override` above their overridden methods?
options: It forces the compiler to run faster.
It is totally optional, but it tells the compiler to check for typos. If you misspell the method name, it will warn you that you aren't actually overriding anything.
questions.explanation: It's an annotation that acts as a safety-net against human typos when replacing parent methods.

questions.question: Which of these lines creates a standard 'Setter' for `private String name;`?
options: public void setName(String incomingName) { this.name = incomingName; }
private void setName(String name) { this.name = name; }
questions.correctAnswer: public void setName(String incomingName) { this.name = incomingName; }
questions.explanation: A Setter is usually `public void` and takes in a parameter to apply to the internal state.

questions.question: Static attributes are reset back to 0 every time a new Object is instantiated from the Class.
questions.explanation: No, they are persistent across the entire life of the application. They are shared universally.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) benefit of Inheritance?
options: You don't have to define a main method.
You can write complex setup logic inside the Parent `Enemy` class ONCE, and all 50 different enemy types inherit it automatically.
You can repeat method names infinitely.
You never have to use variables.
questions.explanation: Centralizing shared logic in a parent class prevents you from typing identical code across 50 different files.

questions.question: The _______ class is the default ultimate parent of ALL objects in Java, whether you use the `extends` keyword or not.
questions.explanation: Every class in Java secretly extends the built-in `Object` class.

questions.question: If `Dog extends Animal`, can `Animal` access methods newly defined inside `Dog`?
options: Yes, parents know everything about their children.
No, Inheritance only flows DOWNWARDS from Parent to Child.
questions.explanation: A parent cannot reach down into their child's specialized behaviors.
